_meta.title: Graph Algorithms I — BFS, DFS, Topo, SCC

questions.question: What is the time complexity of BFS on a graph with V vertices and E edges represented as an adjacency list?
options: O(V²)
O(V log V)
O(V + E)
O(V · E)
questions.explanation: BFS visits each vertex exactly once (O(V) dequeues) and scans each edge exactly once when examining neighbors (O(E) edge scans). The total is O(V + E), assuming an adjacency list representation.

questions.question: During DFS on a directed graph, an edge from vertex u to vertex v is classified as a back edge when:
options: v has already been fully processed (finished) before u discovers it
v is currently on the DFS stack — it is a GRAY ancestor of u
v is a direct child of u in the DFS tree
v has a higher discovery time than u but is not a tree edge
questions.explanation: A back edge points from a vertex to an ancestor still on the recursion stack (GRAY in a 3-color DFS). Back edges are the defining indicator of cycles in directed graphs — a directed graph has a cycle if and only if its DFS produces a back edge.

questions.question: Kahn's topological sort algorithm detects that a graph contains a cycle when:
options: Any vertex has in-degree greater than V/2
The BFS queue empties before all vertices have been added to the ordering
Two vertices have the same in-degree
A vertex is discovered twice during BFS
questions.explanation: Kahn's algorithm enqueues vertices with in-degree 0 and removes them from the graph. If a cycle exists, every vertex in the cycle retains in-degree ≥ 1, so the queue empties prematurely. The check is: if the output order contains fewer than V vertices, a cycle exists.

questions.question: How do Tarjan's and Kosaraju's SCC algorithms differ?
options: Tarjan's uses BFS while Kosaraju's uses DFS
Tarjan's requires O(V²) space for an adjacency matrix; Kosaraju's uses adjacency lists
Tarjan's finds SCCs in a single DFS pass using low-link values on a stack; Kosaraju's runs two DFS passes and requires computing the transpose graph
Tarjan's only works on undirected graphs; Kosaraju's handles directed graphs
questions.explanation: Tarjan's algorithm uses one DFS pass, maintaining a stack and low-link values to identify SCC roots when low[v] == disc[v]. Kosaraju's first DFS records finish order, then runs a second DFS on the transpose graph in reverse finish order — each tree in the second DFS is one SCC. Both are O(V+E), but Tarjan's avoids building the explicit transpose.

questions.question: What does topological sorting produce, and on what type of graph?
options: A shortest path on an undirected graph
A linear ordering of vertices respecting edge directions in a DAG
A minimum spanning tree of a weighted graph
A coloring of a bipartite graph
questions.explanation: Topological sorting orders the vertices of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) so that for every edge (u, v), u appears before v. It is only possible on DAGs — any cycle makes a valid topological order impossible.
